gallery.text.1: Leftover nails and screws and waste timber were used to fashion an initial planter.
gallery.text.2: Leftover polyurethane bonded PVC floating floor underlay was used to make a watertight liner for the planter box.
gallery.text.3: First panter box, made 100% from waste materials, loaded with soild and plants.
gallery.text.4: Home-made die to make rivets from old tie-wire.
gallery.text.5: New hardwood palings and scrap angle iron ready for a new, second generation box.
gallery.text.6: First end panel - board iand angle drilled and rivetted.
gallery.text.7: Second panel under construction. It was clamped in place, and squared up prior to drilling.
gallery.text.8: Completed box. It was reasoned that transverse-alignment of boards would provide greater structural integrity but this is only true if the fasteners are also suitably spec'd.
gallery.text.9: Isometric view of the completed box. The rivets were set against makeshift aluminium washers and peined.
gallery.text.10: Completed box with PVC/polyurethane liner and soil.
gallery.text.11: Third generation plant box in greenhouse, planted out to 'baby beetroot'. This box made use of paired 1/4" screws. The ubiquitous PVC liner is visible.
gallery.text.12: Current generation box. End brackets are of welded angle iron (2" x 1/4" x.s.) drilled and tapped for 1/4" screws, one per paling-end. 6" sections of rebar were welded onto the corners to provide a trivial means of attachement for a PVC conduit structure to support wire mesh or a plastic cover. Simplified assembly consumes less material and provides a sound structure.
gallery.text.13: A view through the greenhouse at the assorted planters and seed boxes.
